title: Wei Li · LTRG — Method Distillation
lede: Method-usage tricks of the Linearized Tensor Renormalization Group (LTRG) as practiced by Wei Li, distilled from the originating paper (Li et al., PRL 2010).

sections.title: Scope, tooling & cost
sections.note: Cases, tooling, and limits are surveyed across the LTRG family (LTRG → XTRG → tanTRG); the tricks below derive from the 2010 LTRG paper.

rows: ==Finite-temperature thermodynamics== — free energy, internal energy, specific heat — of one- and two-dimensional quantum lattice models, reaching the ==thermodynamic limit== (an effectively infinite system) in one dimension.

Benchmarked on one-dimensional XY and Heisenberg chains (this paper); the successors XTRG and tanTRG extend it to ==two-dimensional cylinders of width 8–10== (square-, triangular-, and kagome-lattice magnets) and to the two-dimensional Hubbard model at ==temperatures of a few percent of the hopping energy==.
Two-dimensional systems at finite temperature for which quantum Monte Carlo is obstructed by the ==sign problem== (negative sampling weights).

In two dimensions the accessible ==cylinder width (≈8–10)== is set by the bond dimension $D_c$ rather than the total site count, and quantitative validation is restricted to the ==sign-problem-free regime==.

No general open-source LTRG implementation exists: LTRG and its successors (XTRG, tanTRG) are distributed only in the authors' QMagen package (MATLAB), and are otherwise ==implemented in-house== on a tensor / SVD library. The controlling parameters are intrinsic to LTRG — the bond dimension $D_c$ (retained states) and the imaginary-time step.

A one-dimensional core is ==≈300–800 lines== on a tensor / SVD library (≈1.5–3k for full XTRG / tanTRG). The principal difficulties are ==maintaining a stable truncation toward low temperature== and implementing symmetry-resolved tensor contractions.

Each imaginary-time step scales as $O(D^6 D_c^3)$ — $D$ = ==local state dimension== (2 for spin-½), $D_c$ = bond dimension. Total cost ≈ this × number of imaginary-time steps × number of temperature points. ==Absolute core-hours are not reported== and remain an interview target.

sections.title: Method-usage tricks
sections.note: Techniques for executing the method reliably.

rows: The transfer-matrix network is decimated ==one row per imaginary-time step==, discarding only $D_c$ singular values per step — versus the older coarse-graining renormalization (TRG), which discards substantially more.
Origin of LTRG's ==accuracy and scalability== over TRG.

Singular values are rescaled by their largest element at each step to ==prevent divergence== of the imaginary-time evolution; the free energy is reconstructed from the accumulated normalization factors.
==The discarded norms encode the free energy==.

Independent variation of the imaginary-time step and the bond dimension $D_c$ isolates the error source: at ==high temperature== it saturates in $D_c$ (time-discretization–limited); at ==low temperature== it decreases with $D_c$ (truncation-limited).
Identifies ==which parameter limits accuracy== in each regime.
Pairwise contraction of $2^p$ matrices over $p$ steps yields $2^p$ sites — here $2^{100}$, ==effectively the thermodynamic limit== — at a cost growing only with $p$.
==Removes finite-size error== at negligible cost in one dimension.

A Trotter–Suzuki decomposition recasts the $D$-dimensional quantum model as a ==classical tensor network in one higher dimension==; the thermal density operator becomes a matrix product operator (a one-dimensional tensor chain).
The internal energy is read from a ==local impurity tensor== inserted in the network, cross-validated against the temperature derivative of the free energy.
A measurement intrinsic to the network, ==not finite differencing==.
